channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Midnight Train,’ by Matt Haig
item.description: Matt Haig returns with another life-affirming novel, this one about a man on a magical train that helps him revisit key moments of his life.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan

item.title: Summer’s Best Beach Reads
item.description: If you happen to be near a body of water (salt, fresh, chlorinated or otherwise), here are the books you’ll need.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan

item.title: How Marilyn Monroe Gave a Smart Gloss to Her Image
item.description: It was all about self-improvement for the actress, who was born a century ago next week. Two new volumes shed light on the books she collected and the intellectual she married.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Marilyn and Her Books: The Literary Life of Marilyn Monroe (Book)
The Arthur Miller Tapes: A Life in His Own Words (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Land and Its People,’ by David Sedaris
item.description: In David Sedaris’s wry new collection, being alive is as weird, atrocious, contradictory, unfair and funny as ever.
item.dc:creator: Roddy Doyle

item.title: Robert Daley, Multifaceted Author of ‘Prince of the City,’ Dies at 96
item.description: He wrote 31 books, often drawing on his experiences as a pro football publicist, a foreign correspondent and a gun-toting spokesman for the N.Y.P.D.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.category: Prince of the City: The True Story of a Cop Who Knew Too Much (Book)
item.media:description: Robert Daley in 1997. Six of his books became movies or television treatments, including “Prince of the City.”

item.title: ‘The Maids,’ With Yerin Ha, Asks: Has Life Become One Big Performance?
item.description: In his rethinking of Jean Genet’s classic work about class and power, Kip Williams ponders “a world that gives you every opportunity not to be yourself.”
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Power dynamic: Saban and Ha in Kip Williams’s production, which was rewritten in a contemporary idiom while hewing closely to Genet’s plot.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Freedom Round the Globe,’ by Sarah M.S. Pearsall
item.description: In “Freedom Round the Globe,” Sarah M.S. Pearsall tracks the stirrings of liberty beyond the British colonies that became the United States.
item.dc:creator: Jon Meacham
item.category: Freedom Round the Globe: A World History of the American Revolution (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Babylon, South Dakota,’ by Tom Lin
item.description: Tom Lin’s new novel, “Babylon, South Dakota,” reimagines the western with surreal elements.
item.dc:creator: Marcel Theroux

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Vivisectors,’ by Missouri Williams
item.description: The stoic narrator of “The Vivisectors” befriends a student embroiled in a cultural politics debate.
item.dc:creator: Ann Manov

item.title: Book Review: ‘Trudeau & Doonesbury,’ by Joshua Kendall
item.description: A new biography of Garry Trudeau tracks the rise of a comic strip that brought counterculture and political opinions to the funny pages.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.category: Trudeau & Doonesbury: The Cartoonist Who Turned the News Into Art (Book)
item.media:description: Garry Trudeau in the 1970s, when his comic strip “Doonesbury” was exploding in popularity.

item.title: Book Review: ‘America, U.S.A.,’ by Eddie S. Glaude Jr.
item.description: In “America, U.S.A.,” Eddie Glaude Jr. looks back at the country’s past anniversaries with skepticism and pain.
item.dc:creator: Richard Kreitner
item.category: America, USA: How Race Shadows the Nation's Anniversaries (Book)
item.media:description: The Ku Klux Klan parading in 1926.

item.title: As Draco Malfoy on Broadway, Tom Felton Draws a Crowd
item.description: Fans are traveling great distances for the chance to meet Tom Felton, who has revived a now grown-up Draco Malfoy on Broadway in “Harry Potter and The Cursed Child.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Vidhya Nagarajan
item.category: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child (Play)

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Danger to Be Sane,’ by Rosa Montero
item.description: In “The Danger to Be Sane,” the journalist Rosa Montero delves into the connections between psychic turbulence and creative work — including her own.
item.dc:creator: Carmela Ciuraru
item.category: The Danger to be Sane: Creativity and the Eccentric Mind (Book)
item.media:credit: George C. Beresford/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Virginia Woolf is but one of the poets and writers Rosa Montero profiles in her highly personal study.

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Continues, This Time Onstage at the Public Theater
item.description: This 1993 memoir, which became a film with Winona Ryder and Angelina Jolie, is now a play with songs by Aimee Mann. Here’s how the latest iteration came to be.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: From left, Sally Shaw, Mia Pak, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, King Princess and Ta’Rea Campbell in “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: What to Read This Summer: NYT Reading Bucket List
item.description: Read along with the Book Review this summer: Can you check off five items before fall arrives? (This year, there are prizes!)
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Books Staff

item.title: Book Review: ‘Dekonstructing the Kardashians,’ by MJ Corey
item.description: In “Dekonstructing the Kardashians,” MJ Corey takes America’s most famous TV clan seriously.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Bennett
item.category: Dekonstructing the Kardashians: A New Media Manifesto (Book)

item.title: New Picture Book Biographies of Marcel Marceau, Pablo Casals and John Cage
item.description: As these biographies show, Marcel Marceau, Pablo Casals and John Cage all used silence to great effect.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Burgess
item.category: The Not So Quiet Life of Marcel Marceau (Book)
Music and Silence: The Passion and Protest of Pablo Casals (Book)
Silence Sounds Simple: A Day in the Life of John Cage (Book)
item.media:description: From “The Not So Quiet Life of Marcel Marceau.”

item.title: Mac Barnett Faces Blowback for His Criticism of Children’s Books
item.description: Mac Barnett, the national ambassador for young people’s literature, published a manifesto aimed at adults. Then came the blowback.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.category: Make Believe: On Telling Stories to Children (Book)
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fellow children’s book authors took issue with Mac Barnett for points he made in his new book, “Make Believe.”

item.title: Submit Your Summer Reading Bucket List
item.description: Tell us what you read this summer, and enter the drawing for this year’s prize.

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: Talking Classics: The Shock of the Old (Book)
Keeper of My Kin: Memoir of an Immigrant Daughter (Book)
How to Rule the World: An Education in Power at Stanford University (Book)
Dangerous, Dirty, Violent, and Young: A Fugitive Family in the Revolutionary Underground (Book)

item.title: Publishing’s Latest Piracy Problem: Audiobooks on YouTube
item.description: Illegal, synthetically narrated copies of “The Hunger Games,” hit self-help books and everything in between are increasingly common on the platform.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Gothic Fantasy Novels Filled With Dark Magic
item.description: The best-selling author Rachel Gillig recommends books that bring the haunted hallmarks of Gothic fiction into enchanted settings.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Gillig

item.title: The A.I. Threat to Audiobooks
item.description: Artificial intelligence has made pirated audiobooks faster to make and harder to detect. Our reporter Alexandra Alter tells us about the latest threat to the publishing industry.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry
item.category: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone (Book)

item.title: Was a Story That Just Won a Literary Prize A.I.-Generated?
item.description: A respected literary magazine has published an award-winning short story many readers believe to be generated by artificial intelligence. Experts aren’t all so sure.
item.dc:creator: Valeriya Safronova
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times

item.title: Luminous New Historical Fiction
item.description: Our columnist on the month’s best books.
item.dc:creator: Alida Becker

item.title: Book Review: ‘Dog Days,’ by Emily LaBarge
item.description: In her ingenious “Dog Days,” Emily LaBarge writes about a terrifying event without resorting to “the trauma plot.”
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.media:description: “Trauma is a narrative problem,” Emily LaBarge writes.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Glyph,’ by Ali Smith
item.description: Ali Smith’s new novel, “Glyph,” dispenses with subtleties and takes on an age of war and the ghosts left behind.
item.dc:creator: Megan O’Grady

item.title: Book Review: ‘Keeper of My Kin,’ by Ada Ferrer
item.description: A stirring memoir by the Pulitzer Prize-winning historian Ada Ferrer chronicles her family’s life in the aftermath of traumatic rupture.
item.dc:creator: Miguel Salazar
item.category: Keeper of My Kin: Memoir of an Immigrant Daughter (Book)

item.title: Children of Radicals Write to Understand Their Legacies
item.description: Zayd Ayers Dohrn and Harriet Clark on activism, violence, guilt and trying to make sense of their “incomprehensible” early days.
item.dc:creator: John Leland
item.category: Dangerous, Dirty, Violent, and Young: A Fugitive Family in the Revolutionary Underground (Book)
item.media:credit: Sara Messinger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Zayd Ayers Dohrn and Harriet Clark, writers with new books, meeting for the first time in New York.
